nodes.name: giving a kapila cow to a Veda-knowing Brahmin
giving a cow to a Brahmin who knows Brahma
One who performs Vriddhi Shraddha with food only

edges.dst_name: giving a kapila cow to a Veda-knowing Brahmin
giving a cow to a Brahmin who knows Brahma

edges.src_name: One who performs Vriddhi Shraddha with food only

beats.title: Narada's Inquiry on the Nature of Vishnu
beats.summary: The sages ask Suta about how Sanatkumara taught Narada and how they met. Suta describes the four mind-born sons of Brahma (Sanaka, Sanandana, Sanasphumara, Sanatana) who are devoted to Vishnu. They journey to Brahma's assembly on Mount Meru, see the Ganges (Viṣṇupadī), and prepare to bathe. Narada arrives, praises Vishnu with various names, bathes with them, and then asks them about the nature of the Lord, the threefold karma, and the means to please Vishnu. Shaunaka offers obeisance to Vishnu.

beats.title: Narada's Hymn to Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter is a hymn of praise to Vishnu in his various forms and avatars, composed by the sage Narada. It describes the supreme being as the source of all creation, the inner self, and the object of meditation. The hymn recounts Vishnu's avatars such as Kurma, Varaha, Narasimha, Vamana, Parashurama, Rama, and Krishna, and emphasizes the unity of all deities in Vishnu.

beats.title: Sanaka's Discourse on Creation and the Nature of Maya
beats.summary: Sanaka explains to Narada how the supreme Lord Narayana created Brahma, Rudra, and Vishnu from his body for creation, preservation, and destruction. He discusses the nature of Maya as the Lord's power, which causes duality and suffering when seen as separate, but leads to liberation when seen as non-different. The chapter ends with a hymn of praise to Vishnu by Narada and the sages.

beats.title: The Supreme Reality and Creation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses the nature of the supreme reality, the relationship between Vishnu and his Shakti, and the process of creation. It explains how the one supreme being appears as multiple through the superimposition of ignorance, and describes the evolution of Prakriti, Purusha, and Kala, leading to the creation of the universe by Brahma.

beats.title: Cosmology and the Glory of Bharata
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the creation of the lower worlds (Patala), the seven continents and oceans, and the special status of Bharata-varsha (India) as the land of karma and liberation. It emphasizes the importance of devotion to Vishnu and Shiva, and the practices that lead to liberation.

beats.title: Glorification of Bharata and Vasudeva
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana extols the greatness of Bharata (India) as the land of karma, where righteous actions lead to liberation. It emphasizes that those born in Bharata should perform dharma, dedicate actions to Vishnu, and avoid evil deeds. The chapter also glorifies Vasudeva (Krishna) as the ultimate reality pervading all existence.

beats.title: The Importance of Faith and Devotion
beats.summary: Sanaka asks about the importance of faith (śraddhā) and devotion (bhakti) in spiritual practice. The sage explains that all dharmas, desires, and even liberation are achieved through faith and devotion. Without devotion, rituals, austerities, and gifts are fruitless. Devotion to Hari is the essence of life, like water for living beings. One who follows proper conduct and is devoted to Hari attains the supreme abode.

beats.title: The Glory of Devotion and the Vision of Mārkaṇḍeya
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses the importance of conduct (ācāra) and devotion (bhakti) to Hari (Viṣṇu). It emphasizes that without proper conduct, even Vedic knowledge or pilgrimage cannot purify a fallen person. Devotion is the supreme means to attain liberation, and it arises through association with saints (satsaṅga). The chapter also narrates a vision of Mārkaṇḍeya witnessing the cosmic form of Viṣṇu lying on a banyan leaf after the dissolution of the universe.

beats.title: The Penance of Mrikandu and the Gods' Appeal to Narayana
beats.summary: The sages ask Suta about how Hari alone remained during the great deluge, and Suta narrates the story of sage Mrikandu's intense penance at Shalagrama, which alarmed the gods. The gods, led by Indra, seek refuge in Narayana, praising him with hymns. Narayana appears and reassures them that Mrikandu's penance will not harm them.

beats.title: Mṛkaṇḍu's Vision of Viṣṇu
beats.summary: The sage Mṛkaṇḍu, after performing severe penance, is visited by Lord Viṣṇu. The Lord appears before him in a divine form, and Mṛkaṇḍu offers a hymn of praise. Pleased, Viṣṇu grants him a boon. The chapter emphasizes the power of devotion and the grace of the Lord.

beats.title: The Birth and Early Life of Mārkaṇḍeya
beats.summary: Sanaka narrates to Narada the story of Mārkaṇḍeya's birth and early life. Mṛkaṇḍu, after severe penance, receives a boon from Viṣṇu to be born as his son. Mṛkaṇḍu marries, has a son Mārkaṇḍeya, who is raised with Vedic education and devotion to Viṣṇu.

beats.title: The Cosmic Vision of Markandeya
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the sage Markandeya's penance, his vision of Vishnu during cosmic dissolution, and the revelation of cosmic time cycles (yugas, kalpas, etc.). Markandeya, son of Mrikandu, performs severe austerities to please Acyuta (Vishnu), who grants him the boon to compose a Purana. During the pralaya (cosmic flood), Markandeya floats on the waters for eons, witnessing Vishnu's cosmic sleep. After Vishnu awakens and recreates the world, Markandeya praises him. The chapter also details units of time from nimesha to the lifespan of Brahma.

beats.title: The Characteristics of a Bhagavata
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the characteristics of a true devotee (Bhagavata). It begins with a hymn to Janardana (Vishnu) by the sage Markandeya, followed by the Lord's response that He protects His devotees. Markandeya then asks about the signs of a Bhagavata, and the Lord enumerates various qualities such as compassion, non-violence, detachment, service to parents and gurus, devotion to Vishnu, and recitation of Puranas.

beats.title: Qualities of the Highest Devotees and the Story of Markandeya
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the qualities and actions that characterize the highest devotees (Bhagavata Uttama). It emphasizes service to cows and Brahmins, pilgrimage, chanting Hari's names, planting gardens, building tanks and wells, devotion to Shiva and Vishnu, and equanimity. The chapter concludes with the story of Markandeya receiving a boon from Lord Shiva and attaining liberation.

beats.title: The Glory of Prayaga and Ganga
beats.summary: Narada, having heard the glories of devotion to Bhagavan from Sanaka, asks him to describe the best holy place. Sanaka responds by extolling the confluence of Ganga and Yamuna at Prayaga, the supreme tirtha, and the greatness of Ganga, Tulasi, and devotion.

beats.title: The Glory of Ganga and Kashi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana extols the glory of the Ganga river, the city of Varanasi (Kashi), and the non-duality of Vishnu and Shiva. It describes the purifying power of Ganga's name, bath, and sight, and the liberation attained by remembering Avimukta (Kashi) at death. The chapter also lists other sacred rivers and emphasizes that Ganga is supreme among them, just as Vishnu pervades all.

beats.title: The Glory of the Ganges and the Unity of the Trimurti
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses the non-duality of the Trimurti (Brahma, Vishnu, Shiva), the supremacy of Vishnu, the sacredness of the Ganges and Gayatri, and the importance of Purana recitation and Tulasi devotion. It emphasizes that Hari (Vishnu) pervades all forms and that the Ganges and Gayatri are supreme purifiers.

beats.title: The Fall of King Bāhu Due to Pride and Envy
beats.summary: Sanaka explains to Narada the story of King Bāhu, who ruled righteously but fell into pride and envy, leading to his downfall. The chapter warns against the dangers of arrogance, envy, and attachment to wealth and youth.

beats.title: The Downfall of King Bāhu Due to Envy
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses the destructive effects of envy (asūyā) and pride (ahaṅkāra) through the story of King Bāhu, who lost his kingdom due to these vices. After defeat, he retreats to the forest with his pregnant wife, witnesses a miraculous scene of birds entering a lake, and eventually dies. The chapter emphasizes that envy leads to downfall, while humility and devotion to Viṣṇu bring prosperity.

beats.title: Aurva Prevents a Queen's Sati
beats.summary: Sage Aurva prevents Queen Bāhupriyā from committing sati, revealing she carries a future emperor in her womb. He teaches her about dharma, karma, and the universality of death, leading her to accept her fate and praise the compassionate sage as a form of Viṣṇu.

beats.title: The Birth of Sagara and the Power of Satsanga
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of two queens who serve the sage Aurva. The elder queen, jealous of the younger, poisons her, but due to the sage's grace, the poison is neutralized. The younger queen gives birth to a son named Sagara, who is raised by the sage and becomes a virtuous and powerful king. The chapter extols the power of association with the wise (satsanga) and service to great souls.

beats.title: Sagara's Quest for His Father and the Defeat of Enemies
beats.summary: Sagara, son of Bahu, asks his mother about his father. She tells him that his father was killed by enemies. Sagara vows to destroy them, receives blessings from sage Aurva, then goes to his family guru Vasishtha, obtains divine weapons, and defeats the enemies. The enemies seek refuge with Vasishtha, who transforms them into barbarian tribes (Shakas, Yavanas, etc.) with shaved heads and long hair, excluding them from Vedic rites.

beats.title: Sagara's Anger and Vasistha's Wisdom
beats.summary: Sagara, enraged by his sons' disrespect towards Vasistha, prepares to kill them. Vasistha pacifies him with teachings on karma, non-violence, and the illusory nature of the body, leading Sagara to renounce his anger. Vasistha then performs Sagara's coronation.

beats.title: The Wicked Sāgaras and the Refuge in Kapila
beats.summary: King Bāhu's son Asamañja is wicked, causing distress to the gods. The gods seek refuge in Kapila, who is praised as an incarnation of Viṣṇu and a master of Sāṅkhya-Yoga. Kapila explains that those who seek to destroy others' fame, strength, wealth, and life are the ones who torment the worlds.

beats.title: The Destruction of Sagara's Sons by Kapila's Curse
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the story of King Sagara's sons who, while searching for the stolen horse of their father's Ashvamedha sacrifice, encounter the sage Kapila in meditation. Blinded by pride and anger, they attack the sage, who then burns them to ashes with his yogic power. The chapter includes Kapila's teachings on the nature of wealth, pride, and the inevitable downfall of the wicked.

beats.title: Sagara's Sons and Kapila's Curse
beats.summary: King Sagara's sons are burned to ashes by Kapila's anger for disturbing his meditation. Sagara, though grieved, accepts the divine will and adopts his grandson Aṃśumān as son. Aṃśumān pleads with Kapila, who forgives and prophesies that Aṃśumān's grandson will bring the Ganges to redeem the ancestors.

beats.title: The Story of King Saudasa and the Rakshasi Tigress
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana narrates the story of King Saudasa (also known as Mitrasaha), a descendant of Bhagiratha. After killing a tigress who was actually a rakshasi, he incurs the wrath of her husband, a rakshasa. Fearing revenge, he abandons hunting. Later, he performs a horse sacrifice with sages led by Vasishtha.

beats.title: The Curse of King Saudāsa
beats.summary: King Saudāsa, deceived by a rākṣasa disguised as his guru Vaśiṣṭha, offers human flesh to Vaśiṣṭha. Vaśiṣṭha curses him to become a rākṣasa. The king's wife Madayantī calms his anger, and he redirects the curse-water to his own feet, becoming Kalmāṣapāda. Vaśiṣṭha regrets his lack of discernment.

beats.title: The Curse of King Mitrasaha
beats.summary: King Mitrasaha, cursed to become a rakshasa, wanders in the forest eating humans. He encounters a sage with his wife, kills and eats the sage despite the wife's pleas. The wife curses him to die when he engages in sexual union.

beats.title: The Curse of Saudasa and the Brahmarakshasa's Tale
beats.summary: A brahmin woman curses her husband Saudasa to become a rakshasa for killing her husband. Saudasa then curses her to become a pisachi. They wander to the Narmada river bank where a brahmarakshasa named Somadatta explains that his own fall was due to disrespecting his guru. Saudasa asks about the nature of a guru, and Somadatta begins to describe the qualities of a true guru.

beats.title: The Hierarchy of Gurus and the Power of Puranas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses the hierarchy and importance of gurus, emphasizing that the teacher of the Puranas is the highest guru. It recounts the story of a brahmarakshasa (a cursed brahmin) who explains to King Kalmashapada the consequences of disrespecting a guru, and the supreme value of listening to the Puranas for spiritual liberation.

beats.title: Liberation of the Cursed Demons by Garga
beats.summary: A brahmin named Garga from Kaliṅga, carrying Gaṅgā water and praising Viśveśvara, encounters a piśācī (female goblin) and a rākṣasa (demon) who had been cursed. They ask for liberation. Garga sprinkles Gaṅgā water mixed with tulasī leaf on them, transforming them into divine beings. The rākṣasas explain the power of Hari's name and Gaṅgā water. King Kalmāṣapāda regains his original form.

beats.title: Liberation of King Kalmashapada and Origin of Ganga
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the story of King Kalmashapada (Mitrasaha) who, after being cursed, is liberated by the grace of the Ganga and Vishnu. It includes teachings on the power of Ganga's name and the importance of devotion and compassion. The chapter also transitions to the origin of the Ganga river, narrated by Sanaka to Narada.

beats.title: The Battle of Devas and Daityas and Bali's Conquest
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the great battle between the devas and daityas (asuras) led by Bali. The daityas initially prevail, causing the devas to flee and hide on earth in human forms. Bali, devoted to Narayana, conquers the three worlds and performs ashvamedha sacrifices to please Vishnu, reinstating Indra and the devas in their positions.

beats.title: Aditi's Penance and the Daityas' Defeat
beats.summary: Aditi performs severe penance to regain Indra's sovereignty and defeat the Daityas. The Daityas, disguised as gods, try to dissuade her but fail. When they attack, a fire from her penance burns them, but she is protected by Vishnu's Sudarshana chakra.

beats.title: The Glory of Devotees and Aditi's Vision of Vishnu
beats.summary: Sanaka explains to Narada the invincibility of devotees of Hari (Vishnu). He describes how wherever a devotee resides, all deities, sages, and auspiciousness are present; no harm from fire, thieves, diseases, ghosts, or demons can touch them. The chapter then narrates how Aditi, protected by Vishnu's chakra, was not burned in a forest fire, and Vishnu appeared before her. Aditi offers a hymn of praise to Vishnu, acknowledging his supreme nature.

beats.title: Aditi's Hymn and the Promise of Vamana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana contains a hymn of praise to the supreme deity (Vishnu/Krishna) by Aditi, the mother of the gods. It describes the cosmic form of the Lord, from whom the four varnas and various deities originate. Aditi prays for protection of her sons from the daityas (demons) and expresses her maternal compassion even for the demons. The Lord is pleased and promises to be born as her son (Vamana avatar) and grants a boon that those who recite this hymn will never lose their wealth and children.

beats.title: Aditi's Prayer and the Promise of Vamana Avatar
beats.summary: Aditi asks Lord Janardana how she can bear (hold) him, the supreme being, in her womb. The Lord explains that those who are free from passion and envy, devoted to him, compassionate, devoted to Shiva, engaged in listening to his stories, chaste wives devoted to their husbands, those who serve parents and gurus, who are fond of holy places and good company, who are benevolent, who avoid others' property and women, who worship Tulasi and chant his name, who protect cows, who avoid accepting gifts and are generous, they bear him always. He promises Aditi that he will be born as her son. He then disappears. Aditi, pleased, returns home and in due time gives birth to a son named Vamana, who is Hari himself, holding conch and discus, calm, with a moon-like face, and worshipped by sages. Kashyapa praises him.

beats.title: Vamana's Approach to Bali's Sacrifice
beats.summary: Kashyapa praises Lord Vishnu in his Vamana avatar, who then assures him of fulfilling his wishes. Meanwhile, the demon king Bali begins a grand sacrifice, and Vishnu, as Vamana, approaches the sacrificial ground. The sage Shukra warns Bali not to give anything to Vamana, recognizing him as Vishnu in disguise.

beats.title: Vamana Visits Bali's Sacrifice
beats.summary: Bali Maharaja, while performing a sacrifice, is visited by Lord Vishnu in the form of a dwarf brahmana (Vamana). Bali welcomes him with great devotion and offers him whatever he desires. Vamana asks for three paces of land. Bali agrees, despite warnings from his guru Shukracharya.

beats.title: The Glory of Land Donation
beats.summary: The chapter is a discourse by Lord Vishnu (as Śrībhagavān) to a demon king (daityendra) on the supreme importance of land donation (bhūmidāna). It emphasizes detachment from worldly possessions, the inner presence of the Lord, and the immense spiritual rewards of giving land to a worthy brāhmaṇa, especially one who is poor or without livelihood. The chapter includes a story about a brāhmaṇa named Bhadramati from the Brahmakalpa.

beats.title: Bhadramati's Lament and the Gift of Land
beats.summary: Bhadramati, a poor brahmin with many children, laments his poverty and decides to seek land donation. He goes to the city of Kaushambi and requests five hastas of land from the wealthy brahmin Sughosha, who gladly gives it, reciting a mantra to please Vishnu.

beats.title: The Vamana Avatar and the Binding of Bali
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana narrates the story of King Bali and the Vamana avatar of Vishnu. It describes the gift of land by Bhadramati to a Brahmin, the boon of Vishnu's abode, and then shifts to the Vamana episode: Bali's vow to give three paces of land, Vishnu's cosmic expansion, the piercing of the cosmic egg, the origin of the Ganges, and the praise by gods. Bali is bound and granted the netherworld.

beats.title: Vamana's Boon to Bali and the Glory of Ganga
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses the story of Vamana (Vishnu) binding the demon Bali and granting him the underworld, followed by a discourse on the qualities of a worthy recipient of charity (dana-patra). It also extols the glory of the Ganges river.

beats.title: Dialogue between Bhagiratha and Dharmaraja on the Merits of Giving
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses the types of gifts (dana) and their fruits based on the recipient's qualities and the donor's intention. It emphasizes that gifts given to unworthy recipients (sinners, those who sell the Vedas, etc.) are fruitless, while gifts given with faith and to a worthy recipient are best. The chapter also introduces a dialogue between King Bhagiratha and Dharmaraja (Yudhishthira) about the merits of giving.

beats.title: Bhagiratha's Inquiry on Dharma
beats.summary: King Bhagiratha asks Dharmaraja (Yudhishthira) about the nature of dharma, the rewards for righteous deeds, and the punishments for sins. Dharmaraja explains the immense merit of supporting Brahmins, building water tanks, and providing water to travelers, emphasizing that such acts lead to heavenly rewards and liberation from sins.

beats.title: The Meritorious Lake of Buddhisagara
beats.summary: King Virabhadra of Gauda, a righteous ruler, goes hunting and finds a dried-up lake. With his minister Buddhisagara, he restores it as a reservoir for travelers. After death, Buddhisagara is judged by Dharmaraja and Citragupta, and is granted a celestial vehicle for his meritorious deed.

beats.title: The Merit of Digging Tanks and Building Temples
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the story of a king who, through the advice of his minister, digs a tank (reservoir) over several lifetimes, accumulating merit. The narrative details the successive efforts of various beings (a boar, a king, etc.) to create a water source, culminating in the king's ascension to a celestial chariot. It then transitions to a discourse on the merits of building temples for deities like Shiva and Vishnu, promising heavenly rewards and eventual liberation.

beats.title: Merits of Constructing Temples, Tanks, and Planting Tulasi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the meritorious results of constructing temples, tanks, wells, gardens, and performing acts of devotion like planting Tulasi and applying Urdhva Pundra. It emphasizes that even the poor can attain great merit through humble offerings, and that all such acts lead to the supreme abode of Vishnu.

beats.title: Merits of Bathing Vishnu and Shiva
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the merits of bathing (snāna) Lord Vishnu or Shiva with various substances like milk, ghee, coconut water, etc., on specific auspicious days such as Ekadashi, Purnima, and during eclipses. It promises liberation (moksha) or residence in Vishnu's or Shiva's abode for the performer and their ancestors.

beats.title: Merits of Devotional Acts
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the merits of various devotional practices such as bathing the Shiva linga, offering lamps, food, water, and other gifts to Vishnu and Shiva. It emphasizes the purifying power of these acts, promising liberation from sins and attainment of the divine abodes, often along with one's ancestors.

beats.title: The Merits of Charity and Protection
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana extols the virtues of various acts of charity and protection, particularly towards Brahmins, the sick, and the learned. It describes the spiritual rewards for giving cows, land, food, clothing, and other gifts, emphasizing that such deeds lead to liberation and residence in the abodes of Vishnu, Rudra, or Brahma. The chapter also highlights the supreme merit of giving a Shalagrama stone or a Shiva linga.

beats.title: Merits of Donations and Devotion
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses the merits of various donations and acts of devotion, emphasizing the spiritual benefits of giving Śālagrāma stones, gems, elephants, horses, and other items. It also extols the virtues of serving cows and Brahmins, controlling the senses, and worshiping Śiva and Viṣṇu, promising liberation and heavenly rewards.

beats.title: The Rewards of Temple Worship
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the immense spiritual benefits of various acts of worship and service in temples, such as cleaning, plastering, lighting lamps, making offerings, playing musical instruments, and performing circumambulations. It emphasizes that these actions, done with devotion, lead to liberation and heavenly rewards.

beats.title: Dharmaraja's Discourse on Purification
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana (part 55) presents Dharmaraja's discourse on dharma, focusing on purification rituals (prāyaścitta) for various forms of defilement, including contact with outcastes, bodily impurities, and improper actions. It prescribes specific penances such as fasting, chanting Gayatri, and using pañcagavya.

beats.title: Expiations for Various Sins
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana (Part 56) details various expiations (prāyaścitta) for sins such as contact with outcastes, killing cows, improper sexual relations, and consuming forbidden food. It prescribes specific penances like kṛcchra, cāndrāyaṇa, and fire-immolation, and lists classes of people considered outcastes.

beats.title: Rules of Expiation and Purification
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses various rules and expiations (prāyaścitta) for sins, including improper consumption of oil and medicines, shaving of hair for men and women, penalties for kings who obstruct dharma, and purification rituals involving cow products and kuśa water. It also covers the importance of iṣṭa and pūrta acts, and the consequences of touching impure beings.

beats.title: Rules of Purification and Ancestral Rites
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses rules of purification (sutaka) after birth and death, procedures for shraddha (ancestral rites), and the classification of shraddha into five types. It also describes the method of offering water to ancestors (tarpaṇa) and the importance of water in rituals.

beats.title: Enumeration of Hells and Punishments
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana enumerates various hells (narakas) and the torments (yatanas) suffered by sinners. It lists specific punishments for different sins, including those for mahapatakins (great sinners) such as brahmahatya (killing a brahmin), surāpāna (drinking liquor), steya (theft), and gurutalpaga (incest with guru's wife). The chapter also describes the types of sinners who incur these punishments.

beats.title: Enumeration of Sins Equivalent to the Great Sins
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana enumerates various sins equivalent to the five great sins (mahapataka): brahmahatya (killing a brahmana), surapana (drinking liquor), steya (theft), and gurutalpa (incest with guru's wife). It lists actions that are considered equal in gravity to these sins, such as slandering brahmanas, eating with shudras, stealing various items, and sexual misconduct. The chapter concludes by stating that for some sins, like hating a brahmana or killing a trusted friend, there is no expiation.

beats.title: Consequences of Sin and Hellish Punishments
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the severe karmic consequences and hellish punishments for various sins, including betraying trust, associating with Buddhists or heretics, criticizing the Vedas, stealing, slandering, adultery, and obstructing charity. It details the cycle of rebirths and torments in hell for such sinners.

beats.title: Descriptions of Hells
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes various hells (narakas) and the punishments for specific sins, including killing a Brahmana, cow, or Kshatriya; listening to blasphemy; atheism; looking with anger at Brahmins; betraying trust; breaking boundaries; eating others' food; accepting gifts; reading constellations; eating offerings to deities; abandoning five great sacrifices; giving up worship; imposing excessive taxes; approving seizure of Brahmana villages; wasting semen; brushing teeth on a fast day; abandoning one's duties; and stealing divine or guru's property. The punishments involve boiling in oil, being pierced, eating filth, etc., lasting for eons.

beats.title: Karmic Consequences of Sins
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the severe karmic consequences of various sins, including stealing divine or guru property, reciting Vedas in the presence of women, polluting temples or water bodies, false testimony, breaking vows, and obstructing religious acts. It details the specific hells and rebirths that sinners undergo, emphasizing the principle of karma and the importance of righteous conduct.

beats.title: Sins, Punishments, and the Path to Liberation through Devotion
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes various sins and their corresponding punishments in hell, emphasizing the importance of expiation through penance, devotion to Vishnu, and righteous actions. It categorizes devotion (bhakti) into three types (tamasic, rajasic, sattvic) with subcategories, and highlights that actions dedicated to Vishnu bear fruit, while those not dedicated are fruitless.

beats.title: Bhagiratha's Penance and the Descent of Ganga
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses the nature of devotion (bhakti) and its relationship with dharma. It describes different types of sattvic devotion, emphasizes the importance of combining bhakti with one's own dharma and good conduct, and declares the non-difference between Vishnu and Shiva. The sage Sanaka then narrates how Dharmaraja instructed King Bhagiratha to bring the Ganga to earth to purify his ancestors, leading to Bhagiratha's penance.

beats.title: Bhagiratha's Quest for the Ganga and Bhṛgu's Teachings
beats.summary: King Bhagiratha, seeking to bring the Ganga to earth to purify his ancestors, approaches sage Bhṛgu in his hermitage. Bhṛgu advises him on righteous conduct, truthfulness, non-violence, benevolence, avoiding evil company, performing virtuous deeds, and worshiping Viṣṇu with the dvādaśākṣara or aṣṭākṣara mantra to attain peace and liberation.

beats.title: Bhṛgu's Discourse on Dharma and Mantra
beats.summary: Bhṛgu instructs a king on dharma, truth, non-violence, and the nature of Viṣṇu, culminating in the revelation of the eight-syllable and twelve-syllable mantras for liberation.

beats.title: The Penance of King Nānmāpuṭa and the Appearance of Viṣṇu
beats.summary: King Nānmāpuṭa performs severe penance on Mount Himavat, worshiping Nārāyaṇa. The gods, frightened by the fire from his penance, pray to Viṣṇu. Viṣṇu appears before the king, who prostrates and praises him.

beats.title: Bhagiratha's Praise of Shiva
beats.summary: Lord Vishnu, as the inner controller, appears to King Bhagiratha and instructs him to worship Shiva (Shambhu) as his own form, promising that the king's ancestors will be liberated. Bhagiratha then offers a hymn of praise to Shiva, describing his attributes and forms.

beats.title: The Descent of Gaṅgā
beats.summary: King Bhagīratha praises Śiva with hymns, and Śiva appears, grants him the boon of bringing Gaṅgā to earth to liberate his ancestors. Gaṅgā descends, purifies the ashes of Sagara's sons, and they attain Viṣṇuloka. The chapter extols Gaṅgā's power to destroy sins.

beats.title: Narada inquires about Vishnu's vows
beats.summary: Narada, having heard the glory of Ganga, asks Sanaka about the vows that please Vishnu. Sanaka explains the importance of devotion and describes a specific vow to be observed on the twelfth day of the bright fortnight of Margashirsha, involving fasting, worship, offerings, and charity.

beats.title: The Twelve-Monthly Vratas for Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the ritual of observing the twelve-monthly vows (dvadashi vratas) dedicated to Lord Vishnu in his various forms (Keshava, Madhava, Govinda, Vishnu). It details the specific procedures for each month, including fasting, worship, offerings, donations to Brahmins, and the resulting spiritual benefits, such as the fruits of major sacrifices like the Pundarika, Agnishtoma, Vajapeya, and Gomedha yajnas.

beats.title: The Twelve Dvadashi Vratas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the rituals and benefits of observing the twelve-day vows (dvadashi vratas) in each month of the Hindu calendar, from Vaishakha to Bhadrapada. Each vow involves fasting, bathing the deity with specific substances (milk, ghee, etc.), performing homa with mantras, offering food and dakshina to brahmins, and keeping vigil. The rewards include the fruits of various yajnas and liberation from sins.

beats.title: The Observance of the Twelve Sacred Days (Dvadashi Vrata)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the ritual observance (vrata) of the twelve sacred days (dvadashi) in the months of Bhadrapada, Ashvina, Kartika, and Margashirsha, dedicated to Vishnu in his forms as Hrishikesha, Padmanabha, Damodara, and others. It details the procedures for fasting, bathing, worship, homa (fire offerings), jagarana (night vigil), and charity, along with the associated mantras and fruits. The chapter emphasizes the importance of devotion and the rewards of performing these vows, including liberation and the attainment of Vishnu's abode.

beats.title: Dvadashi Vrata and Introduction to Another Vow
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the procedure for observing the Dvadashi vrata (twelfth-day vow) dedicated to Lord Vishnu, including rituals such as bathing the deity with five nectars, offerings, homa, feeding brahmins, and listening to Puranic stories. It also introduces a new vow in the next chapter that removes sins and grants desires.

beats.title: The Purnima Vrata (Full Moon Vow)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the Purnima Vrata (full moon vow), detailing the rituals, worship of Lakshmi-Narayana, homa, fasting, and the concluding udyapana ceremony. It emphasizes the vow's power to destroy sins and grant all desires.

beats.title: The Ritual of Dhvajāropaṇa and Lakṣmīnārāyaṇa Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the ritual of dhvajāropaṇa (flag-hoisting) in honor of Vishnu, including the procedure, mantras, and benefits. It also briefly mentions the Lakṣmīnārāyaṇa vrata performed on Māghaśīrṣa Pūrṇimā.

beats.title: Ritual of Flag Installation and Vishnu Stuti
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the ritual of installing a flag (dhvaja) in a Vishnu temple, including the worship procedures, hymns of praise to Vishnu, and the subsequent honoring of brahmins and the guru. It emphasizes devotion and the cosmic form of Vishnu.

beats.title: The Glory of Hoisting the Flag of Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the glory of hoisting a flag (dhvaja) in Vishnu's temple. It narrates the story of King Sumati and his wife Satyamati, who were devoted to Vishnu and regularly performed the dhvaja-aropana ritual. The sage Vibhandaka visits them, and the chapter emphasizes the immense purifying power of this practice.

beats.title: The King's Transformation
beats.summary: King Romapada (formerly a shudra named Malin) recounts his past life of sin and his transformation through service to a dilapidated Vishnu temple. He and his wife now perform flag-hoisting and dance worship. The sage Vibhandaka questions this practice, and the king explains his journey from sinner to devotee.

beats.title: The Debate Between Yama's and Viṣṇu's Messengers
beats.summary: A sage recounts his past life as a hunter who lived with a woman named Avakokila, both sinners. After death, Yama's servants came to take them, but Viṣṇu's messengers intervened, arguing that the couple had purified themselves by building a temple. A debate ensues between Yama's and Viṣṇu's servants about dharma and adharma.

beats.title: The Deliverance of Two Sinners by Viṣṇudūtas
beats.summary: The chapter narrates a dialogue between Yamadūtas and Viṣṇudūtas over two sinners who, despite their past sins, are saved due to their unintentional service in Viṣṇu's temple. The Viṣṇudūtas explain that even involuntary acts of devotion, such as sweeping, plastering, lighting lamps, and watering in a temple, can lead to liberation. The sage Vibhaṇḍaka hears this and praises the couple, concluding that service to Hari is like a wish-fulfilling cow, granting supreme bliss.

beats.title: The Haripañcaka Vrata
beats.summary: Sanaka instructs Narada on the Haripañcaka vrata, a five-day fast and worship of Vishnu during the bright fortnight of Mārgaśīrṣa, detailing the rituals, mantras, and benefits.

beats.title: Vrata Descriptions: Haripañcaka and Māsopavāsa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes two vratas (religious observances): the Haripañcaka vrata (a five-day fast) and a month-long vrata performed in specific months. It details the procedures, offerings, and benefits, including liberation from rebirth and attainment of Brahmaloka. The text emphasizes devotion to Vishnu, fasting, charity, and the recitation of the vrata's merits.

beats.title: Merits of Fasting: Māsopavāsa and Ekādaśī
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the merits of various fasting observances, particularly the month-long fast (māsopavāsa) and the Ekādaśī fast. It details the specific fruits (phala) obtained from performing these fasts a certain number of times, comparing them to the fruits of Vedic sacrifices like Aśvamedha, Naramedha, Gomedha, and Brahmamedha. The chapter emphasizes the purifying power of these fasts, especially Ekādaśī, which is dear to Viṣṇu and can destroy even great sins like brahmahatyā. It concludes by stating that hearing or reciting this account brings liberation from all sins.

beats.title: The Glory of Ekadashi Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the sacred vow of Ekadashi, its observances, and the associated rituals. It emphasizes fasting, worship of Vishnu, and the spiritual benefits of Ekadashi, including liberation from rebirth. The chapter also includes a dialogue between Bhadrashila and his father Gulava, and a story about the sage Galava.

beats.title: Bhadraśīla's Past Life and Devotion
beats.summary: The sage Gālava asks his son Bhadraśīla how he attained such extraordinary devotion to Viṣṇu at a young age. Bhadraśīla reveals his past life as King Dharmakīrti, who ruled righteously but later fell into adharma due to association with sinners. He recounts his encounter with Yama, which led to his current birth and devotion.

beats.title: The Redemption of a Sinful King through Ekadashi
beats.summary: A sinful king recounts his past misdeeds and how, after dying on the banks of the Reva river while observing Ekadashi fasting and vigil, he was brought before Yama. Citragupta notes his observance of Ekadashi, which purifies all sins, leading Yama to honor him and instruct his messengers to spare devotees of Vishnu and those who follow dharma, while bringing sinners for punishment.

beats.title: The Redemption of a Sinner through Ekadashi Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the story of a man who, after death, is taken to Yama's court. Yama instructs his messengers to bring those who are averse to Vishnu devotion, engage in evil deeds, and neglect Ekadashi vows. The man, having heard Yama's words, repents and remembers his own sinful actions. Through remorse and listening to dharma, his sins are destroyed, and he attains a form resembling Hari. He then travels to Vishnu's abode in a celestial chariot, resides there for eons, later enjoys Indra's realm, and finally is born in a family of Vishnu devotees with memory of past lives. He resolves to observe Ekadashi vows and worship Vishnu. His father, the sage Galava, rejoices and teaches him the method of Vishnu worship. The chapter ends with Narada asking Sanaka about the duties of varnas and ashramas.

beats.title: Varna Dharma and Kali Yuga Practices
beats.summary: Sanaka explains the duties and customs of the four varnas (Brahmins, Kshatriyas, Vaishyas, Shudras) and the practices to be followed in the Kali Yuga, emphasizing adherence to dharma and avoidance of forbidden acts.

beats.title: Duties and Rituals for Varnas and Ashramas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses the duties and rituals for different varnas (castes) and ashramas (stages of life), emphasizing the importance of following one's own dharma. It details the consequences of adopting another's dharma, the proper times for samskaras (sacraments) like garbhadhana, simantakarma, jatakarma, namakarana, caula, and upanayana, and the importance of performing them with mantras and proper materials. The chapter also highlights that all varnas can attain muni-hood through their prescribed actions, and that following the four ashramas pleases Vishnu and leads to liberation.

beats.title: Rules for Upanayana and Brahmacharya
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana (Part 92) provides detailed instructions on the upanayana (sacred thread ceremony) and the duties of a brahmachari (student). It covers the consequences of delaying the ceremony, the appropriate materials for the sacred thread, staff, and garments for each varna, the daily routine of a student including begging for alms, and prohibitions on certain behaviors. The text emphasizes the importance of serving the guru, performing fire rituals, and avoiding contact with fallen or impure individuals.

beats.title: Rules of Salutation and Auspicious Days
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana (part 93) provides detailed instructions on when and to whom one should not offer salutations (abhivadana), and lists auspicious days (yugadyas, manvadyas) for performing rituals like shraddha. It also warns against studying the Vedas on prohibited days (anadhyaya) and describes the consequences of doing so.

beats.title: Duties of a Brahmana and Marriage Guidelines
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses the duties of a Brahmana, emphasizing the importance of Vedic study and proper conduct. It provides guidelines for marriage, listing qualities to avoid in a bride and describing the eight types of marriage (Brahma, Daiva, Arsha, Prajapatya, Asura, Gandharva, Rakshasa, and Paishacha). The chapter also outlines the attire and accessories a Brahmana should wear during rituals.

beats.title: Conduct for the Twice-Born
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana provides a detailed list of rules and prohibitions for a dvija (twice-born) regarding daily conduct, including dress, eating, social interactions, and ritual practices. It emphasizes purity, avoidance of certain foods and behaviors, and the importance of performing śrāddha and other rites at specific times. The chapter concludes by stating that all religious acts are fruitless without the ūrddhvapuṇḍra (vertical mark on the forehead).

beats.title: Instructions on Householder Conduct
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana (part 96) focuses on the daily conduct (sadācāra) for householders, particularly brahmins. It details rules for bodily functions, purification (śauca), and proper times for activities, emphasizing that purity of mind and body is essential for spiritual progress.

beats.title: Daily Purification Rituals
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana provides detailed instructions on daily purification rituals, including tooth-cleaning (dantakashtha), bathing (snana), and sandhya (twilight worship). It specifies the types of wood for tooth-sticks, the procedure for bathing with mantras invoking sacred rivers and tirthas, and the method for performing sandhya with nyasa and invocation of Gayatri and other deities. The chapter emphasizes the importance of cleanliness and mantra recitation for spiritual purification.

beats.title: Instructions on Sandhya and Gayatri Japa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana provides detailed instructions on the daily rituals (sandhya) for a brahmana, including the Gayatri mantra, meditation on the three goddesses (Gayatri, Savitri, Sarasvati), and the importance of performing these rites without fail. It emphasizes the consequences of neglecting sandhya and outlines the proper procedure for japa, offerings, and subsequent duties like bathing and brahmayajna.

beats.title: Householder Duties and Stages of Life
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana provides detailed instructions on householder duties, including worship of deities, hospitality to guests, the five great sacrifices (pañcayajñas), proper eating conduct, and the progression to the forest-dwelling (vānaprastha) and renunciation (sannyāsa) stages of life. It emphasizes daily rituals, purity, and devotion to Nārāyaṇa.

beats.title: Rules for Ascetics and Śrāddha Procedure
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the rules and conduct for ascetics (yatis) and the proper procedure for performing śrāddha rituals. It emphasizes detachment, meditation on Nārāyaṇa, and the importance of inviting qualified brāhmaṇas for śrāddha.

beats.title: Rules for Śrāddha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana lists various types of individuals who are unfit to be invited to śrāddha ceremonies, including those with physical deformities, immoral behavior, or lack of devotion to Viṣṇu and Śiva. It then provides detailed instructions on the proper timing, procedure, and rules for performing śrāddha, emphasizing the importance of the kutapa period (afternoon) and the correct selection of brahmins.

beats.title: Ritual Instructions for Shraddha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana provides detailed instructions for performing the shraddha ritual, including the invocation of the Vishvedevas and pitrs, offerings of water, barley, and food, and the proper use of mantras and fire. It emphasizes the importance of following the prescribed procedures and addresses contingencies such as the absence of the sacred fire.

beats.title: Śrāddha Vidhi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana provides detailed instructions for performing śrāddha rituals to honor the ancestors (pitṛs). It emphasizes the importance of offering food to Viṣṇu, reciting specific mantras, and avoiding certain actions to ensure the ancestors are satisfied and the performer gains spiritual merit.

beats.title: Rules for Tithis in Rituals
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses the rules for determining auspicious tithis (lunar days) for performing rituals, especially śrāddha and vratas. It emphasizes that all existence is pervaded by Vishnu, and that proper observance of tithis leads to spiritual benefits.

beats.title: Rules for Auspicious Times
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses the auspicious times (tithis, sankrantis, etc.) for performing religious rites such as snana, dana, japa, and shraddha. It provides detailed rules for determining the correct tithi when there is overlap or diminution, emphasizing the importance of the prevailing tithi at specific times of day. The text also covers the classification of amavasya into sinivali and kuhu, and the proper observance of ekadashi.

beats.title: Determination of Tithis and Penance
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses the determination of Ekadashi and Dwadashi tithis (lunar days) for fasting, including rules for when these tithis are mixed or incomplete. It also covers exceptions during solar eclipses, lunar eclipses, and other special occasions, and emphasizes the importance of following dharma as per the Vedas to attain Vishnu's supreme abode.

beats.title: Expiatory Rites for Sins
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana details the expiatory rites (prāyaścitta) for various sins, particularly focusing on the penance for killing a Brahmin (brahmahatyā). It prescribes specific vows, durations, and practices for purification, emphasizing devotion to Hari (Vishnu) and the importance of proper expiation for spiritual cleansing.

beats.title: Penances for Wine and Gold Theft
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses penances (prāyaścitta) for drinking wine (surā) and stealing gold (suvarṇa). It details the types of intoxicating drinks, the purification rituals for different amounts of stolen gold, and the conditions under which a twice-born (dvija) can be cleansed of these sins.

beats.title: Expiations for Sins
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses various expiations (prāyaścitta) for sins such as theft of gold and silver, incest, and association with sinners. It prescribes specific penances like the Brahmahatyāvrata, Cāndrāyaṇa, and fire rituals, emphasizing purification through disciplined practice.

beats.title: Expiatory Rites and the Power of Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana details various expiatory rites (prāyaścitta) for sins such as killing animals, consuming forbidden items, and contact with impure persons. It emphasizes the supreme purifying power of remembering and worshipping Lord Vishnu, which can absolve even the greatest sins.

beats.title: The Glory of Vishnu Bhakti and the Path of Yama
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana (Part 111) emphasizes the supreme importance of devotion (bhakti) to Vishnu (Hari) for liberation. It contrasts the fleeting nature of worldly life with the eternal benefit of worshiping Vishnu, stating that even great sinners can attain the highest state through devotion. The chapter also introduces a dialogue between Narada and Sanaka about the terrifying path of Yama for sinners.

beats.title: The Path of Sinners and the Righteous
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the contrasting experiences of sinners and virtuous souls on the path to Yama's abode. Sinners endure immense suffering, while the righteous enjoy comforts based on their meritorious deeds such as charity, service, and recitation of scriptures.

beats.title: Yama's Judgment and the Fate of Sinners
beats.summary: Yama, in his four-faced form holding conch, discus, and sword, addresses the souls. He praises those who perform meritorious deeds and warns sinners. He describes the hierarchy of beings, emphasizing the importance of dharma and the inevitability of facing one's karma. Yama's messengers, led by Citragupta, terrify the sinners and cast them into hell.

beats.title: The Fate of Karma After Cosmic Dissolution
beats.summary: Narada asks Sanaka about the fate of karma after cosmic dissolution. Sanaka explains that the supreme Lord Narayana, though attributeless, appears as Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva to create, sustain, and dissolve the universe. After dissolution, he recreates the world as before. Karma, both good and bad, must be experienced and never perishes. The chapter then describes the cycle of birth in stationary beings (plants) and various animal forms, detailing the suffering involved.

beats.title: The Suffering of Samsara and the Path to Liberation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the cycle of birth and suffering in samsara, detailing the journey of the jiva through various yonis, the painful experience in the womb, childhood, youth, old age, and death. It emphasizes the rarity of human birth and the importance of seeking liberation through devotion to Vishnu and the cultivation of jnana.

beats.title: The Means to Cut the Bonds of Samsara
beats.summary: Sanaka explains to Narada that the ultimate means to cut the bonds of samsara is devotion to Vishnu, who is the supreme mokshada. He describes Vishnu as the inner controller, the support of all, and the one who grants liberation to those who worship him with detachment.

beats.title: Sanaka Instructs Narada on Kriya Yoga
beats.summary: Sanaka explains to Narada that liberation is attained through knowledge rooted in devotion, and that Kriya Yoga (devotional practice) is essential for success in Jnana Yoga. He describes the forms of Vishnu to be worshipped, the qualities required for a yogi, and emphasizes that mere external worship without inner purity is futile.

beats.title: Kriya-Yoga and the Path to Liberation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana defines Kriya-Yoga as the practice of worshiping Vishnu through fasting, listening to Puranas, and offering flowers, leading to purification and liberation. It discusses the distinction between the perishable world and eternal Hari, the importance of detachment, and the realization of the non-dual Brahman through knowledge and yoga.

beats.title: Discourse on Maya and the Eight Limbs of Yoga
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana (part 119) presents a discourse on the nature of Maya (illusion) and the path of Yoga, specifically the eight limbs of Yoga (Ashtanga Yoga). It explains that Maya is neither real nor unreal but indescribable, and that through Yoga one can overcome ignorance and realize the supreme Brahman. The sage then enumerates and defines the Yamas (restraints) and Niyamas (observances) as foundational practices for Yoga.

beats.title: Discourse on Japa, Purity, and Yoga
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses the three types of japa (vocal, whispered, mental), the importance of inner purity (bhāvaśuddhi) over external rituals, the practice of contentment (santoṣa), and the necessity of a pure mind for spiritual success. It also lists various āsanas for yoga practice.

beats.title: Yogic Practices for Liberation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana describes the practice of pranayama (breath control) and its role in yoga, including the nadis (ida, pingala, sushumna), the four types of pranayama (recaka, puraka, kumbhaka, shunyaka), and the importance of sense control (pratyahara) and meditation (dhyana) on Vishnu for liberation.

beats.title: Yoga-Nirupana: Exposition of Meditation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana (part 122) expounds the practice of meditation (dhyana) and the realization of the supreme Brahman. It describes the process of focusing the mind on the form of Vishnu, the significance of the Pranava (Om), and the attainment of liberation through steady meditation. The text emphasizes the unity of the meditator, meditation, and the object of meditation, leading to the realization of the non-dual, blissful Self.

beats.title: Sanaka's Instruction on Bhakti Yoga
beats.summary: Sanaka instructs Narada on the supremacy of devotion to Vishnu (Janardana) as the means to liberation, describing the characteristics of a true devotee, the three states of consciousness (waking, dreaming, deep sleep) as manifestations of the inner controller, and the practices that please the Lord, such as non-violence, truthfulness, celibacy, chanting His name, and association with the good.

beats.title: Discourse on Devotion to Hari
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana is a discourse by a sage (likely Narada) to another sage (Devarṣi) emphasizing the importance of devotion to Vishnu (Hari) over worldly attachments. It critiques ego, desire, and the fleeting nature of life, urging constant worship of Hari to escape the cycle of samsara. The text highlights that even a caṇḍāla (outcaste) who is a devotee of Vishnu is superior to a brāhmaṇa without devotion.

beats.title: The Urgency of Devotion to Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana is a discourse on the importance of devotion to Vishnu (Hari) and the dangers of worldly attachments. It emphasizes that life is fleeting and one should worship Vishnu to attain liberation. The text repeatedly stresses the power of chanting the names of Vishnu, the need to abandon anger, desire, and envy, and the futility of material pursuits without devotion.

beats.title: The Story of Vedamali and His Sons
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses the importance of devotion to Hari (Vishnu) and contrasts divine and demonic creation. It narrates the story of the Brahmin Vedamali, who accumulated wealth through improper means, and his twin sons Yajnamali and Sumali.

beats.title: The Renunciation of Vedamali
beats.summary: A wealthy man named Vedamali, after amassing wealth through improper means, realizes the futility of desire and greed. He distributes his wealth, performs charitable acts, and retires to the forest to practice penance. There he meets the sage Jananti, who teaches him about the dangers of desire and the path to liberation.

beats.title: The Instruction of Vedamālin
beats.summary: In this chapter, the sage Jānanti instructs the disciple Vedamālin on the path to liberation through devotion to Viṣṇu, ethical conduct, and self-inquiry. Vedamālin, after receiving initial guidance, becomes confused about his true identity and returns to Jānanti for deeper clarification. Jānanti explains that the sense of 'I' and 'mine' is an illusion, and the true self is beyond name, form, and action.

beats.title: The Liberation of Vedamali and the Conflict of His Sons
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana discusses the nature of the supreme Brahman as attributeless, self-luminous, and beyond action. It narrates the story of the sage Vedamali, who after being instructed by his guru, realizes the non-dual self and attains liberation. It also describes the conflict between his sons Yajñamali and Sumali, highlighting the consequences of attachment to wealth and sensual pleasures.

beats.title: The Fate of Two Brothers: Yajñamālī and Sumālī
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of two brothers, Yajñamālī and Sumālī. Sumālī squanders his brother's wealth, indulges in wine and meat, and is abandoned by family. He dies a sinful death and is tormented by Yamadūtas. Yajñamālī, a devotee of Viṣṇu, dies and is taken to Viṣṇuloka in a divine chariot. On the way, he sees Sumālī suffering and asks Viṣṇudūtas how to free him. They promise to reveal the means.

beats.title: The Power of Temple Cleaning: Yajñamālī and Sumālī
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of Yajñamālī, a sinful Vaiśya named Viśvaṅghabhara, who accidentally cleans a temple floor during a rainstorm, earning merit. He dies from a snakebite and is reborn as a brāhmaṇa with unwavering devotion to Hari. Later, he helps his brother Sumālī attain liberation by transferring the merit of cleaning a small area of temple floor. Both brothers ultimately reach Viṣṇuloka, with Yajñamālī attaining final liberation and Sumālī eventually being reborn as a virtuous brāhmaṇa who attains liberation after bathing in the Gaṅgā and seeing Viśveśvara. The chapter extols the power of temple cleaning and devotion to Hari.

beats.title: The Redemption of Gulika
beats.summary: This chapter from the Narada Purana extols the glory of Vishnu worship, emphasizing that even the worst sinners can attain liberation through devotion. It narrates the story of the hunter Gulika, who despite his heinous deeds, was saved by accidentally uttering the name of Narayana.

beats.title: The Hunter and the Sage
beats.summary: A hunter attempts to steal from a temple but is stopped by the sage Uttanka, who teaches him about dharma, karma, and the futility of greed.
